// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 8 — THE DANGEROUS MIDDLE: WHEN CLAUDE IS RIGHT AND WRONG SIMULTANEOUSLY

This chapter gives a working overview of The Dangerous Middle: When Claude Is Right and Wrong Simultaneously, focusing o

Chapter 8 — The Dangerous Middle: When C — The hardest moment in a grading-tool build: Claude produces feedback that passes every handoff condition you wrote and i
NARRATION

This chapter gives a working overview of The Dangerous Middle: When Claude Is Right and Wrong Simultaneously, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, What the data actually shows, The narrowing principle, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. The hardest moment in a grading-tool build: Claude produces feedback that passes every handoff condition you wrote and is still pedagogically wrong.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING

OPENING

10.3%
scoring gap exists between high-proficiency ESL essays and n
→
25%
in the same kind of NLP-based grading systems Seth was build
NARRATION

CLAUDE.md was tight. The grading-workflow Skill from Chapter 6 ran cleanly. The Hook from Chapter 7 blocked numeric grades. The handoff conditions from Chapter 4 caught the scope errors. The five capacities from Chapter 5 fired when they should. Twenty-three submissions, twenty-three flag reports in grading/flags/. Cohort patterns. Verified. Ready. Seth opened three of the per-student flag reports as a final spot-check.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT THE DATA ACTUALLY SHOWS

WHAT THE DATA ACTUALLY SHOWS.

14%
disagreement is not random
→
10.3%
lower than equivalent-quality native-speaker essays on DeBER
→
25%
on AAVE work, per a 2024 AI Ethics Institute study of 10,000
·High-proficiency ESL essays score 10.3% lower than equivalent-quality native-speaker essays on DeBERTa-v3 rubric assessm
·NLP-based grading systems underperformed by up to 25% on AAVE work, per a 2024 AI Ethics Institute study of 10,000+ assi
·Stanford 2024 analyses of AI-detection tools (GPTZero and equivalents) found false-positive rates 2–4× higher for ESL wr
NARRATION

Three years ago, LLM grading-accuracy literature reported rubric-grading correlations of roughly 0.55 with human raters. Some earlier versions of this book quoted those numbers. They are no longer current. Multiple 2024, 2025 studies show LLM rubric-grading correlations of 0.86 to 0.93 with human raters on rubric-based tasks. Yavuz (2025) in the British Journal of Educational Technology reports correlations in this range for EFL essay grading. arXiv:2505.01035 (May 2025) found four major LLMs maintained accuracy even with simplified rubrics. The ACL BEA Workshop 2025 collects similar findings.

// SLIDE 04 — THE NARROWING PRINCIPLE

THE NARROWING PRINCIPLE.

The chapter's central operational move.

The narrowing principle — Claude does pattern detection across submissions. The teacher writes per-student feedback.
NARRATION

Claude does pattern detection across submissions. The teacher writes per-student feedback. The before-state: Claude generates individual student feedback directly from each submission. This is where the ESL and AAVE bias surfaces, because the per-student generation operates against rubric criteria that the model applies in a population-blind way. The output is fluent, technically rubric-compliant, and pedagogically harmful for the students whose linguistic backgrounds fall outside the rubric's assumed register.

// SLIDE 05 — THE EQUITY HANDOFF CONDITION

THE EQUITY HANDOFF CONDITION.

·No flag report uses normative language about the student's grammar that would not also apply to a native speaker writing
·No flag report recommends "grammar review" as a primary intervention for a student whose primary issue is content or str
·No cohort pattern names a linguistic feature (preposition use, copula presence) as a "problem" when the feature is a mar
·The teacher reviews flag reports for ESL and AAVE students manually before approving for use in feedback drafting.
NARRATION

A new kind of handoff condition for grading work, added to your project's vocabulary. The standard handoff condition asks: did the step produce what was specified? The equity handoff condition asks: did the step treat all students' language as equally valid?

// SLIDE 06 — TWO MORE DANGEROUS MIDDLE PATT

TWO MORE DANGEROUS MIDDLE PATTERNS.

Beyond the bias case, two patterns the discipline must defend against.

Two more dangerous middle patterns — The "first draft never final" violation. The grading-workflow Skill says feedback is first draft only; the Hook blocks n
NARRATION

Beyond the bias case, two patterns the discipline must defend against. The "first draft never final" violation. The grading-workflow Skill says feedback is first draft only; the Hook blocks numeric grades; CLAUDE.md says the teacher reviews before sending. Then time pressure hits at 9 PM on a Sunday. The teacher reads the drafts, finds them mostly fine, sends them. The discipline that survives the time pressure is the one the chapter is for.

// SLIDE 07 — TEACHER BUILD: INSTRUMENT YOUR

TEACHER BUILD: INSTRUMENT YOUR GRADING TOOL AGAINST THE DANGEROUS MIDDLE

Restructure to the narrowing principle. Modify your grading-workflow Skill (Chapter 6)Claude detects patterns and flags, never generates per-student feedback. You write the per-student feedback from the fla
·Add equity handoff conditions. Three to five for your population. Encode what can be automated as a Hook; leave the rest
Add the Hook. PreToolUse hook on Write to grading/sent/block any file lacking the explicit marker. Add a small Skill that creates the marker (one-key invocation) so the discip
Add the directive-feedback check. Before any feedback you write goes out, apply the Hattie three-question gateDoes this feedback specify what the student should do differently? Is the action tied to something they are learning? Wi
·Update CLAUDE.md. Add the equity rules. Add the directive-feedback gate as a Lessons Learned entry. The persistent conte
NARRATION

Apply the chapter's discipline to your build. Restructure to the narrowing principle. Modify your grading-workflow Skill (Chapter 6): Claude detects patterns and flags, never generates per-student feedback. You write the per-student feedback from the flags.

// SLIDE 08 — CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS A

CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS AUDIT AI-GENERATED FEEDBACK FOR THE THREE FAILURE MODES

Does any feedback show bias toward ESL or AAVE writing?→Does any feedback read as final rather than draft?→Does any feedback describe without directing?
NARRATION

Provide a set of AI-generated feedback drafts (you can produce them from your own tool, or use a curated set). Some are clean; some exhibit the bias pattern; some are descriptive-not-directive; some violate first-draft-only by reading as final. Each student audits the set against the three patterns:

// SLIDE 09 — COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

14%
disagreement clusters in the pedagogically high-stakes cases
→
48.9%
finding is the reality; the discipline is the recommendation
NARRATION

"Higher accuracy means safer to delegate." No. The 7, 14 percent disagreement clusters in the pedagogically high-stakes cases. Better accuracy on the easy cases does not address the cases that matter. "My rubric is unbiased." Rubrics that score "standard grammar" or "appropriate register" can systematically disadvantage ESL and AAVE students even when no human applying the rubric would do so. The bias is in how the model implements standard, not in the rubric's intent.

// SLIDE 10 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Alfred Binet (1857–1911) — French psychologist who developed the first intelligence test (1905, with Théodore Simon) and

AI Wayback Machine — The dangerous middle in AI-assisted grading is Binet's warning applied to LLM rubric assessment. The model produces flag
NARRATION

Alfred Binet (1857, 1911) , French psychologist who developed the first intelligence test (1905, with Théodore Simon) and who, almost immediately upon its publication, warned against using it as a fixed measure of a child's capacity. Binet's insistence , articulated in Les idées modernes sur les enfants (1909) , was that the test was an instrument, not a verdict. The instrument produced a number. The number required human interpretation to become a judgment. The interpretation was the teacher's, informed by the score; the score was not the judgment.

// SLIDE 11 — BRIDGE

BRIDGE

gap is from controlled studies of high-proficiency ESL essay

NARRATION

The dangerous middle is named. Chapter 9 introduces the Claude Code feature that manages context across complex builds , Subagents. : DeBERTa-v3 evaluation reported in multiple 2024, 2025 ESL grading-accuracy studies; the 10.3 percent gap is from controlled studies of high-proficiency ESL essays at equivalent human-rated quality. : AI Ethics Institute (2024). Study of 10,000+ assignments scored by NLP-based grading systems; AAVE-using submissions underperformed by up to 25 percent on rubric scoring. : Yavuz, F.
